project_overview.project_name: South Sudan Resilient Livestock Sector Project (RLSP)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To enhance animal health, improve rangeland management, and strengthen resilience of livestock production in project areas.
project_overview.beneficiaries: 166,000 direct beneficiary households (69% of targeted area population); 400 community animal health workers (CAHWs); government agencies; consumers and traders of animal-derived foods (indirect beneficiaries). Expected livestock population: 2,355,518 cattle; 1,324,741 sheep; 1,628,711 goats.

primary_locations: Mundri West and Mundri East (Western Equatoria State)
Tonj East and Tonj North (Warrap State)
Bor County and Twic East (Jonglei State)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - South Sudan is classified as a fragile state with ongoing conflict. The project operates in FCV context with sustained risk of instability and violence. Localized conflicts, intercommunal violence, herder-farmer conflicts over grazing land, and spillover effects from Sudan conflict are documented. Competition for grazing land and farm encroachment fuels herder-farmer conflicts.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Strengthening animal health and welfare systems
key_components.description: Provides animal health services including vaccinations and parasite control using One Health approach. Includes capacity building for community animal health workers (CAHWs), construction/rehabilitation of veterinary clinics, vaccination centers, quarantine centers, mini laboratories for disease screening, rehabilitation of Marial Lou Livestock Training Center, and implementation of livestock vaccination and deworming campaigns. Targets priority cattle diseases (CBPP, HS, BQ, Anthrax) and small ruminant diseases (PPR, S&G pox, CCPP, Anthrax).

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Sustainable and integrated rangeland development and management for livestock production, with focus on feed and water management
key_components.description: Improves management of rangelands and water resources critical for livestock. Includes capacity building for sustainable rangeland management, fodder production and conservation techniques (silage, hay making), climate-smart water harvesting infrastructure (solar-powered boreholes, hafirs), construction of 16 multi-purpose solar-powered boreholes, 24 water points, 16 fodder conservation infrastructures, establishment of 40 water users' associations, distribution of 4,000 fuel-efficient stoves, and 40,000 drought-resistant tree seedlings for ecosystem restoration.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Technical Assistance, Institutional Strengthening, and Project Management and Coordination
key_components.description: Supports Ministry of Livestock and Fisheries (MLF) in reviewing and developing policies and regulations to strengthen sector governance. Includes technical assistance for policy formulation, development of livestock bills and protocols, Performance of Veterinary Services (PVS) assessment, support for IGAD Protocol on Transhumance, project management, coordination, M&E, grievance redress mechanism, and third-party monitoring.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Livestock and Fisheries (MLF) as lead implementing agency. Project Coordination Unit (PCU) established within MLF with qualified staff including Project Coordinator, Financial Management Specialist, and Procurement Specialist.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Advisory Committee chaired by Minister responsible for Livestock and Fishery; National Technical Committee chaired by MLF; Project Coordination Unit (PCU) for day-to-day implementation. Hybrid implementation model with FAO providing technical expertise on specific activities (vaccination procurement, administration). Coordination with development partners including FAO, AfDB, IGAD, VSF Canada, ICRC. Community participatory approach involving MLF, state/county authorities, and South Sudan Pastoralist General Union (SSPGU).
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for Investment Project Financing (February 2025, Sixth Edition). Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) and 18-month initial procurement plan prepared. Procurement activities implemented through PCU. UN agencies engaged on specific activities with World Bank no-objection required. Procurement risk rated High due to incomplete public procurement framework, weak PFM systems, narrow dry season window (6 months), significant delays in processing, nascent market, high transportation costs, and weak procurement staff capacity.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning (MEL) system to track progress toward PDO. Baseline, midterm, and endline surveys conducted. Digital tools including Geo-Enabling initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) and Power BI. Third-Party Monitoring Agent (TPMA) engaged to monitor implementation progress and verify results. Quarterly and bi-annual reporting. M&E capacity building at all levels. Project results framework with PDO indicators and intermediate indicators tracked bi-annually or quarterly.

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak state capacity, sporadic civil war, and intercommunal violence render South Sudan's national institutions less functional. 2018 Peace Agreement intended to lead to 2024 general election postponed to 2027. Fragile socioeconomic situation, elite capture, and discrimination based on ethnicity, gender, and religion pose high risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: UN and development partners with solid fiduciary, technical, and operational capacity will participate as implementation partners. Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan (SRAMP) prepared. Risk assessment conducted in all project locations to identify risks and fault lines. Close collaboration with beneficiary communities. Flexibility in project implementation to adapt to changes.

identified_risks.risk_description: Ongoing conflict and violent landscape in South Sudan. Localized conflicts, intercommunal violence, herder-farmer conflicts over grazing land. Spillover effects from Sudan conflict causing mass animal migrations and displacement. Limited access to some project areas during certain seasons.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan (SRAMP) prepared prior to effectiveness and updated to respond to security situation changes. Site-specific Area Security Plans prepared and adopted prior to commencement of work. Narrow window of opportunity (6 months dry season) for implementation. Community engagement and conflict-sensitive approach. Mapping of pastoralist routes to support transit agreements and promote sustainable natural resource use.

identified_risks.risk_description: High inflation, significant exchange rate variation, declining oil revenues (70% of exports disrupted by Sudan conflict), low collection of non-oil revenues. Economic contraction for fourth consecutive year (FY24: -8.2%). Rupture of oil pipeline during Sudan fighting severely disrupted oil exports and foreign exchange earnings.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IMF Staff Monitored Program supporting reforms in fiscal discipline, monetary and exchange rate reform, debt management, and governance strengthening. World Bank PFM project supporting robust budget controls and procedures. Move toward macro stability will facilitate project implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Ministry of Livestock and Fisheries has limited capacity for World Bank project implementation (first engagement since independence). Weak financial management and procurement capacity. Inadequate budgeting capacity, manual accounting systems, inadequate staffing, lack of World Bank project experience. Weak internal controls, no fixed assets management system, inadequate segregation of duties. Significant delays in disbursements and external audits.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project Coordination Unit (PCU) established with recruited qualified consultants including FM Specialist, Procurement Specialist, and other specialists. FM arrangements handled by PCU headed by FM Specialist consultant supported by project accountant. Capacity building and training on World Bank regulations and PPDAA Act 2018. FAO providing technical support on specific activities. Third-Party Monitoring Agent (TPMA) engaged. Hybrid implementation model with UN agencies. Designated Account (DA) for fund management.

identified_risks.risk_description: Market volatility and ongoing conflict could affect fiduciary arrangements and impede access to beneficiaries and secure delivery of project inputs. Weak public procurement, legal, regulatory, and financial management frameworks. Limited FM systems and capacity at MLF. Weak internal control environment. Significant delays in external audits by National Audit Chamber (NAC).
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implementation through FAO and TPMA leveraging their experience and capacity in fragile contexts. PCU establishment with qualified staff. FM assessment conducted with recommendations implemented. Procurement manual to be prepared. World Bank Anticorruption Guidelines applied. Statement of Expenditures (SOE) submission required quarterly. Annual audits by qualified auditors. Designated Account for fund segregation.

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk of Gender-Based Violence (GBV) and Sexual Exploitation, Abuse, and Harassment (SEAH) in target counties. Limited experience and capacity of MLF on social risk management (first WB project). Risks of social exclusion, elite capture, insufficient community engagement, limited participation of women/youth/vulnerable groups, child labor, GBV/SEAH. Labor influx and supervision challenges. Risks related to land acquisition, physical and economic resettlement. Inadequate grievance redress mechanisms.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: SEAH Action Plan prepared detailing comprehensive risk assessment, SEAH requirements in bidding documents, GBV risks and mitigation in contractor ESMPs, mapping of GBV/SEAH service providers, community and worker sensitization, Codes of Conduct for staff and workers, referral pathways to GBV providers, GBV expertise in PCU. Labor Management Procedures (LMP) annexed to ESMF. Layered Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) at national, state, and local levels with capacity building. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) for land acquisition and access/use restrictions. Inclusive consultations and community engagement. Gender-sensitive GRM with anonymous reporting channels and survivor-centric approach. Awareness raising on gender equality and GBV. Women participation targets (40%) in beneficiaries and leadership positions.

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental risks from agro-pastoral activities, construction of small-scale infrastructure, water infrastructure, soil restoration activities. Risks of pollution, GHG emissions, biodiversity loss, disease, land consumption, water use, waste disposal, agrochemical and veterinary medicine management, occupational health and safety, air and noise emissions. Potential for overutilization of natural resources and soil degradation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) with rigorous site selection criteria prepared and disclosed. Collaboration between local and national authorities to enforce regulatory measures supporting sustainable practices (controlling overgrazing, equitable water use). Community-based natural resource management programs for ownership and stewardship. All infrastructure designed to climate-resilient standards. Solar-powered infrastructure (boreholes, vet clinics, minilabs). Hafirs as rainfed systems not channeling Nile water. Fuel-efficient stoves reducing fuel consumption by 20-50%. Afforestation with 40,000 seedlings for carbon sequestration. Hazardous Waste and Pest Management Plans. Monitoring and reporting on E&S risk management implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Livestock sector suffers from chronic underinvestment and lacks overarching strategic and policy guidance due to unfunded mandates. Limited sector ownership due to inadequate budget allocation (0.25-0.79% of national budget over 3 years). Pending bills delaying policy implementation. Weak governance structure with inadequate human resource capacities and overlaps in roles.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project becomes platform to strengthen government capacity and leadership in livestock sector. Component 3 supports operationalization of relevant sector policies. World Bank leverages convening role to bring development partners together for collaboration and coherence. Technical assistance for policy formulation and implementation. Support for enactment of pending bills (Animal Health Bill, Veterinary Bill, Meat and Hygiene Bill, Livestock Bill). Performance of Veterinary Services (PVS) assessment. Support for IGAD Protocol on Transhumance.

identified_risks.risk_description: Success hinges on marrying economic and cultural norms to create equitable ownership. Fluid and fragile context requires flexibility in implementation. Risk of exclusion, fraud, and corruption. Need for continuous risk monitoring and sensitization.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Flexibility in project implementation essential to adapt to changes. Continuous risk monitoring and assessment. Sensitization activities. Rigorous GRM to address concerns of exclusion, fraud, corruption. Involvement of SSPGU. Integrated approach combining One Health, climate change, rangeland management, and policy alignment. Community participatory approach. Conflict-sensitive design.

identified_risks.risk_description: South Sudan's long history of conflict may affect engagement with local authorities and communities. Multiple actors in implementation could cause substantial risk without clear leadership and coordination. Potential for conflicting interests among pastoralists, farmers, and government.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Consensus-based, robust implementation arrangements defining role of each actor. Consistent and inclusive consultations. Flexibility in project implementation to adapt to fluid context. Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) disclosed and updated throughout implementation. Community engagement and capacity-building plan. Mapping of pastoralist routes with consultation with states, counties, payams, and SSPGU. Community participatory land use planning (CLUP). Transhumance dialogues to mitigate cattle raiding conflicts.

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High environmental and social risk classification
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (Relevant)
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (Relevant)
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (Relevant)
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (Relevant)
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (Relevant)
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure (Relevant)
OP 7.50: Projects on International Waterways (Yes - exception to riparian notification approved January 23, 2025)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Layered Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) at national, state, and local levels. Gender- and GBV-sensitive GRM with anonymous reporting channels and separate system for GBV-related grievances following survivor-centric approach. Procedural manual and grievance redress structures established to promptly review, address, and document complaints. Capacity building plan for PIU staff, stakeholders, beneficiaries, communities, and project workers on GRM functioning. Ethical, confidential, and safe reporting channels particularly for women and girls reporting SEAH issues. Target: 100% of grievances addressed. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism also available.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: High gender focus with gender lens applied to all activities. Target: 40% female beneficiaries (above NDC targets). Women face serious disadvantages in livestock sector including restrictive cultural norms, limited access to information, exposure to zoonotic diseases through handling raw animal products, and GBV risks. Project addresses: (1) GBV mitigation through awareness, behavior change, provision of fuel-efficient stoves reducing firewood collection exposure, GBV Action Plan within ESMF; (2) Women's participation in vaccination campaigns, infrastructure management, leadership in water users' associations (40% women on boards); (3) Training of female CAHWs and Animal Health Auxiliaries (AAH) with 40% female target; (4) Women-led microenterprises for fodder commercialization and milk processing; (5) Personal Initiative (PI) training for CAHWs to foster entrepreneurial mindsets; (6) Stainless steel milk collection cans for women's economic empowerment; (7) Reduction of cooking time and firewood collection burden through fuel-efficient stoves; (8) Women's financial resilience through improved milk storage and quality; (9) Gender and GBV sensitization for transformative interventions; (10) Involvement of key influencers (male partners, fathers, community leaders) in awareness activities; (11) Nutrition and food safety training emphasizing women's role in household nutrition.
vulnerable_groups: Women and girls (disproportionately affected by food insecurity, malnutrition, GBV, and indoor air pollution)
Displaced persons and refugees (spillover from Sudan conflict)
Children (at risk of child labor, malnutrition, pneumonia from indoor air pollution)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Addresses root causes of conflict including competition for grazing land, water, and fodder through: (1) Strategic development of water and fodder resources to reduce scarcity-driven competition; (2) Community participatory land use planning (CLUP) and mapping of pastoralist routes to support transit agreements; (3) Promotion of sustainable natural resource use among farmers, pastoralists, and livestock herders; (4) Establishment of water users' associations and pasture users' associations for equitable resource management; (5) Support for community-based peace-building mechanisms strengthening livestock-related conflict resolution; (6) Decentralization of decision-making and strengthening of traditional governance systems for managing natural resources and land disputes; (7) Transhumance dialogues to mitigate cattle raiding conflicts; (8) Support for IGAD Protocol on Transhumance promoting safe cross-border movement of livestock; (9) Conflict-sensitive Community Engagement and Capacity-Building Plan; (10) Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan (SRAMP) with site-specific Area Security Plans; (11) Risk assessment in all project locations to identify risks and fault lines; (12) Flexibility in implementation to adapt to conflict dynamics.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan is classified as a fragile state with multiple fragility factors: (1) Protracted conflict since independence in 2011, with civil war 2013-2018 causing 400,000 deaths and 4 million displaced; (2) Ongoing violence at state and subnational levels with localized conflicts and intercommunal violence; (3) Spillover effects from Sudan conflict (began April 2023) causing mass animal migrations, displacement, and refugee influx; (4) Severe economic downturn with -7.2% annual per capita decline (2011-2022) and -8.2% contraction in FY24; (5) Extreme poverty: 75.9% below national poverty line, 67.3% in extreme poverty (2022); (6) Multidimensional poverty quasi-universal (93% experiencing deprivation); (7) Vulnerability to poverty widespread (99.7% poor or likely poor); (8) Acute hunger affecting 47% of population (6.3 million people in IPC Phase 3+); (9) Chronic food insecurity (63.2% severe, 87.3% moderate-severe 2021-2023); (10) High malnutrition and maternal mortality (1,150 per 100,000 live births); (11) Weak state capacity and non-functional institutions; (12) Elite capture and discrimination based on ethnicity, gender, religion; (13) Delayed elections (postponed from 2024 to 2027) creating political uncertainty; (14) High inflation and macroeconomic instability; (15) Dependence on oil revenues (90% of foreign exchange) disrupted by Sudan conflict; (16) Weak governance and regulatory frameworks; (17) Ranked second most vulnerable to natural hazards and climate change globally (2024 INFORM Risk Index); (18) Recurrent floods and droughts causing livestock losses and displacement.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security is a major implementation challenge: (1) Ongoing conflict and violent landscape with localized conflicts and intercommunal violence; (2) Herder-farmer conflicts over grazing land and farm encroachment; (3) Cattle raiding and livestock theft; (4) Spillover effects from Sudan conflict; (5) Limited access to some project areas during certain seasons; (6) Narrow window of opportunity (6 months dry season) for implementation; (7) High risk of GBV and SEAH in target counties; (8) Risks to project staff and beneficiaries. Mitigation: Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan (SRAMP) prepared prior to effectiveness and updated regularly. Site-specific Area Security Plans prepared before work commencement. Close collaboration with beneficiary communities. Risk assessment in all project locations. Flexibility in implementation. Community engagement and conflict-sensitive approach. Involvement of development partners with on-ground security capacity. Transhumance dialogues to mitigate cattle raiding conflicts.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Significant displacement and refugee issues: (1) Historical: Civil war (2013-2018) caused 4 million displaced over nearly 5 years; (2) Current: Spillover from Sudan conflict (began April 2023) causing significant influx of refugees and returnees; (3) Climate-driven displacement: Floods and droughts cause internal displacement of livestock herders; (4) Livelihood-driven displacement: Forced displacement of livestock herders due to animal diseases and resource scarcity; (5) Cross-border movements: Mass animal migrations from Sudan conflict putting pressure on disease management systems at borders. Project addresses displacement through: (1) Improving livestock health to reduce forced displacement of herders; (2) Enhancing resilience of livestock production to climate shocks; (3) Developing water and fodder resources to reduce resource-driven migration; (4) Supporting sustainable natural resource management to reduce conflict-driven displacement; (5) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) for potential land acquisition and access/use restrictions; (6) Support for IGAD Protocol on Transhumance promoting safe cross-border movement of livestock.
